_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Fayette County, KY. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Fayette County was formed in 1780 from Lincoln County while Kentucky was still part of Virginia. Records begin in the 1780s and are among the best-preserved in the state. The county's wealth and Lexington's status as a major city produced extensive records across all categories.

current_courthouse.location: Lexington, KY — 162 E. Main Street (current Robert F. Stephens Courthouse opened 2002; historic courthouse on N. Limestone)

previous_courthouses.location: Lexington, early log and frame courthouses
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as the county grew. Early records survived these transitions. Kentucky was part of Virginia until 1792, so the earliest Fayette County records were created under Virginia law.
Served for most of the antebellum and Civil War periods. No significant fire or war damage. Replaced by a new building.
Replaced by the 1900 courthouse which served through most of the twentieth century.

record_losses.cause: Wear and handling — no major fire or disaster
record_losses.description: Fayette County has no record of a significant courthouse fire or disaster. Records run continuously from the county's 1780 formation — among the earliest county records in Kentucky. Some early loose papers have deteriorated, but the bound volumes survive. The county's continuous record from Virginia-era Kentucky is exceptional.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} was in Fayette County after 1780, the records almost certainly survive. Gaps point to the ancestor's circumstances — transience, poverty, or the fact that enslaved people were recorded as property in deed books and will inventories, not as individuals in their own right.
record_losses.source: Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives. Guide to Kentucky County Court Records.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation. Pre-statehood deeds (1780-1792) recorded under Virginia law.
deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Early Virginia land grants cross-referenced in Virginia Land Office records.
deeds.repository: Fayette County Clerk; Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives (KDLA) microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com Kentucky land records

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — will books and estate inventories from formation. Estate inventories list enslaved people by name, age, and value (1780-1865).
wills_estates.gaps: Loose estate papers from earliest decades fragmentary. Bound volumes intact.
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; some KDLA digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds and consents from formation. Kentucky marriage records are among the best in the South.
marriages.gaps: Early marriage bonds more complete than formal certificates. Ministers' returns sporadic before 1852.

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — County Court order books from formation. District and Circuit Court records also well-preserved.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in the earliest volumes due to wear.

vital_records.completeness: Kentucky began statewide vital registration in 1911. Compliance was spotty until approximately 1915. Lexington city health records may predate 1911.
vital_records.repository: Kentucky Cabinet for Health and Family Services; KDLA for older records

substitute_sources.source_type: Virginia land grants and military warrants
substitute_sources.description: Before Kentucky statehood (1792), Fayette County land was distributed through Virginia land grants and military warrants. These records document the earliest settlers and provide a crucial link between Virginia and Kentucky records.
substitute_sources.repository: Virginia Land Office records, Library of Virginia; Kentucky Secretary of State Land Office; KDLA
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracing the earliest Bluegrass settlers. Virginia military warrants identify Revolutionary War service. Land grants show the chain of title from wilderness to private ownership.
substitute_sources.source: Jillson, Willard Rouse. The Kentucky Land Grants. Filson Club Publications, 1925.

substitute_sources.description: Transylvania University (founded 1780, reorganized 1799) maintained enrollment records, alumni lists, and institutional documents that identify Bluegrass families — particularly the planter and professional classes.
substitute_sources.repository: Transylvania University Special Collections, Lexington, KY
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Identifies families in the Bluegrass elite. Enrollment records, commencement lists, and alumni records place individuals at specific dates.
substitute_sources.source: Wright, John D. Transylvania: Tutor to the West. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 1975.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Slave schedules 1850-1860. Agricultural schedules 1850-1880. Fayette County enumerated from the first Kentucky census (1790).
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. Slave schedules (1850, 1860) list enslaved people by age, sex, and color under slaveholder names. Agricultural schedules document farm size, crop production, and livestock.

narrative_hooks: Fayette County's records run continuously from 1780 — if {ancestor_name} was here, the paper trail almost certainly survives. Gaps say more about the ancestor's status than about courthouse losses.
Before 1792, Fayette County was part of Virginia — {ancestor_name}'s earliest records may be in Virginia land grant files, not Kentucky courthouse books.

Estate inventories in Fayette County listed enslaved people by name, age, and appraised value — if {ancestor_name} was enslaved, the inventory that documented {pronoun_possessive} existence treated {pronoun_object} as property, but it also preserved {pronoun_possessive} name when few other records did.
Kentucky marriage bonds from the 1780s are among the earliest county records in the state — if {ancestor_name} married in Fayette County, the bond likely survives, signed by the groom and his surety.
source_references: Kentucky Department for Libraries and Archives. Guide to Kentucky County Court Records.
Jillson, Willard Rouse. The Kentucky Land Grants. Louisville: Filson Club Publications, 1925.
Wright, John D. Transylvania: Tutor to the West. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky, 1975.
Clark, Thomas D. A History of Kentucky. Lexington: John Bradford Press, 1960.
